title: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium
authors: Anne Marie Valente, Michael J. Landzberg

items.subtopic: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult | PREVALENCE
items.title: Understanding the Growing Prevalence of Adult CHD
items.content: The prevalence of congenital heart disease (CHD) among adults is significant, with an estimated 1.4 million affected individuals in the United States alone. This highlights the importance of recognizing and managing CHD not only in childhood but also throughout adulthood.
keyPoints: At least 1.4 million adults in the U.S. have congenital heart disease (CHD).
>10% of adults with CHD in Europe will be over 60 years old by 2030.
Lifelong follow-up is recommended for adults with CHD, ideally coordinated by clinicians specializing in adult congenital heart disease (ACHD).
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult | PREVALENCE | THE CHANGING LANDSCAPE OF ADULT CHD

items.subtopic: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult | THE CHANGING LANDSCAPE OF ADULT CHD
items.title: The Evolution of ACHD as a Subspecialty
items.content: Over recent decades, adult congenital heart disease (ACHD) has emerged as a distinct subspecialty within cardiovascular care. This evolution is marked by the development of guidelines and certification processes to ensure standardized, high-quality care for adults with CHD.
keyPoints: The field of ACHD has expanded significantly over the past two decades.
Guidelines for adult CHD care were first published in 2008 and revised in 2018 by the American College of Cardiology and American Heart Association.
In 2015, board certification examinations for ACHD were introduced by the American Board of Medical Subspecialties.
Advanced fellowship training requirements for ACHD care were established by the Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult | PREVALENCE | THE CHANGING LANDSCAPE OF ADULT CHD

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.title: Importance of Transition from Pediatric to Adult Cardiology for ACHD Patients
items.content: Pediatric cardiology programs must partner with adult congenital heart disease (ACHD) programs to ensure a successful transition. Gaps in care during this transition are common and may be influenced by social determinants such as race, ethnicity, socioeconomic status, insurance access, and geographic location.
keyPoints: Lifelong care is essential for ACHD patients.
Transition from pediatric to adult cardiology programs is crucial.
Gaps in care during transition are common due to disparities in social determinants of health.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.title: Preoperative Assessment for Noncardiac Surgery in ACHD Patients
items.content: ACHD patients may have limited hemodynamic reserve and subclinical organ dysfunction, necessitating a comprehensive multispecialty assessment before invasive or operative procedures. Anesthetic management should consider the patient's cardiac anatomy, previous interventions, and noncardiovascular organ status.
keyPoints: Most adults with CHD are classified as stage A heart failure or greater.
Limited hemodynamic reserve and subclinical organ dysfunction may be present.
Comprehensive multispecialty assessment is recommended before surgery.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.title: Pregnancy Counseling and Risk Assessment in Women with CHD
items.content: Women with CHD should receive preconception counseling about maternal and fetal risks. The CARPREG 2 registry provides a weighted-risk score for predicting adverse cardiac outcomes during pregnancy, with factors such as prior cardiac events, functional status, and mechanical heart valves being highly significant.
keyPoints: Preconception counseling is crucial for women with CHD.
CARPREG 2 registry offers a weighted-risk score for pregnant women with heart disease.
Risk factors include prior cardiac events (3 points), decreased functional status (NYHA class ≥III) (3 points), and mechanical heart valves (3 points).
Predicted risks for cardiac events: ≤1 point, 5%; 2 points, 10%; 3 points, 15%; 4 points, 22%; >4 points, 41%.
Fetal echocardiogram advised between 18 and 22 weeks of gestation.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.title: Understanding Segmental Approach in Congenital Heart Disease (CHD)
items.content: The segmental approach to diagnosing CHD involves analyzing the heart's principal segments: atria, ventricles, and great arteries. This method helps determine segmental alignments and connections, crucial for understanding complex defects.
keyPoints: Principal segments include atria, ventricles, and great arteries.
Segmental alignment determines what drains into what (e.g., RA aligns with RV).
Connections describe physical links between segments (e.g., PA connected to RV by a muscular conus).
Both alignment and connection are essential for diagnosing complex CHD.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.title: Cardiac Development Timeline and Processes
items.content: The heart begins forming in the third week of gestation and is nearly fully formed by 8 weeks. Key processes include migration of precardiac cells, formation of a linear heart tube, and septation influenced by cardiac neural crest cells.
keyPoints: Heart formation starts in the third week of gestation.
By 8 weeks' gestation, the heart is nearly fully formed.
Cardiac neural crest cells are crucial for outflow septation and semilunar valve formation.
The D-loop results from elongation and looping of the heart tube.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.title: Genetic Considerations in Congenital Heart Disease
items.content: CHD is often multifactorial with genetic links. Trisomy 21 increases CHD risk by 50%, while DiGeorge syndrome (22q11 deletion) is linked to conotruncal defects. Noonan and Williams syndromes also have specific cardiac manifestations.
keyPoints: Trisomy 21 has a 50% association with CHD, often atrioventricular canal defects.
DiGeorge syndrome (22q11 deletion) is linked to conotruncal defects.
Noonan syndrome involves dysplastic pulmonary valves and PTPN11 gene mutations.
Williams syndrome includes supravalvar aortic stenosis and 7q11.23 deletion.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.title: Etiologies of Right Heart Dilation
items.content: Right heart dilation can result from various congenital conditions including valvular anomalies, myocardial anomalies, and shunt lesions. Cardiac imaging is crucial for identifying the specific cause.
keyPoints: Congenital etiologies include Ebstein anomaly, pulmonary regurgitation, arrhythmogenic RV dysplasia, Uhl's anomaly, atrial septal defects (ASD), and partial anomalous pulmonary veins.
Cardiac imaging is essential for determining the cause of right heart dilation.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.title: Management of Atrial Septal Defects
items.content: ASDs can lead to right heart dilation and should be considered for closure if symptomatic or associated with complications. Pulmonary hypertension may develop in up to 10% of cases.
keyPoints: Large ASDs often present in childhood but may not be discovered until adulthood.
Symptoms include exercise intolerance, arrhythmia, and dyspnea with exertion.
Right heart dilation without other etiology in unrepaired ASD is a risk for symptomatic right heart failure and atrial arrhythmias.
Pulmonary hypertension develops in up to 10% of patients with unrepaired ASD.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.title: Partial Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Return
items.content: PAPVR can be discovered incidentally and may require repair based on anatomical variance, symptoms, and surgical experience. Concomitant pulmonary hypertension is uncommon.
keyPoints: Common connections include a left upper pulmonary vein to an ascending vertical vein or right upper pulmonary vein draining to the superior vena cava.
Symptomatology may be absent; repair decisions should consider anatomy, lung ventilation/perfusion, hemodynamic response, symptoms, and surgical experience.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.title: Ebstein Anomaly: Recognizing Physical Examination Findings
items.content: Ebstein anomaly results from embryologic failure of tricuspid valve leaflet delamination, leading to various abnormalities. In adults, the severity of disease influences physical examination findings.
keyPoints: Apical and posterior displacement of the dilated tricuspid valve annulus is characteristic.
Physical exam may show a split first heart sound with a 'sail sign' due to anterior leaflet redundancy.
Significant tricuspid regurgitation can cause prominent 'v' waves in jugular venous pulsations, though often absent due to abnormal right atrial compliance.
ECG abnormalities include right atrial and ventricular enlargement; up to 20% show Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.title: Ventricular Septal Defects: Classification and Clinical Implications
items.content: VSDs are the most common congenital anomaly at birth but less prevalent in adults due to spontaneous closure. Their classification depends on location, influencing clinical outcomes.
keyPoints: VSDs account for ~10% of CHD in adults.
Most common VSD location is in the membranous septum (perimembranous or outlet defects).
Muscular defects often become pressure and flow restricted, with minimal hemodynamic impact.
AV canal (inlet) defects involve abnormalities of AV valve leaflets; subpulmonary (conal) defects may cause aortic insufficiency.
Adults with small VSDs without ventricular dilation or pulmonary hypertension generally have excellent outcomes.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.title: Patent Ductus Arteriosus: Auscultation and Hemodynamic Impact
items.content: PDA involves a connection between the aorta and pulmonary artery, with clinical presentation varying based on size.
keyPoints: Small PDAs may be silent and asymptomatic.
Classic murmur is systolic-diastolic, heard best below the left clavicle.
Large PDAs cause left heart dilation and can lead to elevated pulmonary vascular resistance, including Eisenmenger syndrome (ES) risk.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.title: Tetralogy of Fallot: Surgical Repair and Adult Sequelae
items.content: Surgical repair in infancy is the standard for Tetralogy of Fallot, but adults may experience hemodynamic issues requiring reintervention. Pulmonary regurgitation post-repair leads to RV dilation and dysfunction, which are linked with adverse outcomes.
keyPoints: Tetralogy of Fallot occurs in 0.5 per 1000 live births.
Surgical repair aims to alleviate pulmonary stenosis and close the VSD.
Up to 10% of TOF patients have an anomalous coronary artery.
Pulmonary regurgitation is common after TOF repair, associated with RV dilation.
RV size, function, and mass quantification are crucial post-repair.
Left ventricular dysfunction occurs in at least 20% of adults with repaired TOF.
A QRS duration ≥180 ms on ECG indicates increased risk for ventricular tachycardia and sudden death.
72% survival at 40 years, but only 25% cumulative event-free survival post-repair.
Pulmonary valve replacement is the most common reintervention.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.title: Timing and Techniques for Pulmonary Valve Replacement in TOF
items.content: The optimal timing of pulmonary valve replacement in asymptomatic patients with repaired Tetralogy of Fallot is unclear. While traditionally surgical, percutaneous implantation is increasingly used.
keyPoints: Optimal timing for PVR in asymptomatic repaired TOF patients remains unclear.
PVR has been traditionally surgical but percutaneous methods are becoming more common.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.title: Transposition of the Great Arteries: Surgical Evolution and Long-term Outcomes
items.content: The arterial switch operation is now preferred for D-loop TGA, replacing earlier atrial switch procedures. This change has improved long-term survival but systemic RV dysfunction remains a concern post-atrial switch.
keyPoints: D-loop TGA involves AV concordance and ventricular-arterial discordance.
Atrial switch procedures (Mustard, Senning) were common until the 1980s.
Systemic RV dysfunction is a significant long-term issue post-atrial switch.
The arterial switch operation became preferred in the 1980s for D-loop TGA.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.title: L-loop Transposition of the Great Arteries: Presentation and Complications
items.content: L-loop TGA involves both atrioventricular and ventriculoarterial discordance, resulting in normal arterial oxygen saturation. Patients often have associated congenital anomalies like dextrocardia, ASDs, dysplastic tricuspid valve, and pulmonary stenosis. Conduction disturbances are common, with complete heart block occurring in up to 30% of patients.
keyPoints: L-loop TGA involves AV discordance (RA to LV, LA to RV) and ventriculoarterial discordance (LV to PA, RV to aorta).
Normal arterial oxygen saturation despite anatomical abnormalities.
Associated congenital anomalies: dextrocardia, ASDs, dysplastic tricuspid valve, pulmonary stenosis.
Conduction disturbances common; complete heart block in up to 30% of patients.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 2)

items.title: Coarctation of the Aorta: Clinical Features and Risks
items.content: Coarctation of the aorta presents with lower extremity blood pressure and pulses that are lower than upper extremity values. A continuous murmur over the scapula may be present due to collateral blood flow. Bicuspid aortic valve is commonly associated, and Turner syndrome should be considered in women with specific physical features.
keyPoints: Obstruction typically at descending aorta near PA junction.
Lower extremity BP/pulses lower than upper extremities unless collaterals develop.
Continuous murmur over scapula due to collateral flow.
Bicuspid aortic valve commonly associated.
Consider Turner syndrome in women with short stature, webbed neck, lymphedema, primary amenorrhea.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 2)

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.question: Which factor is NOT considered a high-weighted risk (3 points) for pregnant women with heart disease according to the CARPREG 2 registry?
options: Prior history of cardiac events or arrhythmias
Decreased functional status (New York Heart Association class ≥III)
Presence of a mechanical heart valve
items.explanation: Late pregnancy assessment is assigned 1 point, not 3 points.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.question: What should be done with ACE inhibitors in women with CHD who are planning to become pregnant?
items.explanation: Alternatives should be considered, as ACE inhibitors are teratogenic and contraindicated during pregnancy.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.question: What is the recommended timing for a fetal echocardiogram in pregnant women with CHD?
items.explanation: A fetal echocardiogram is advised between 18 and 22 weeks of gestation.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.question: Which of the following is a reason for regular evaluations in adults with CHD?
options: To monitor anatomic, hemodynamic, and electrophysiologic sequelae
items.explanation: Regular evaluations are advised for surveillance of anatomic, hemodynamic, and electrophysiologic sequelae.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.question: Which classification system approach does the chapter follow for describing congenital heart lesions?
items.explanation: The chapter follows a segmental approach to describe the congenital heart lesions.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.question: What is the normal alignment of the right atrium in the heart?
items.explanation: In the normal heart, the right atrium is aligned with the right ventricle.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.question: Which cells are essential for septation of the outflow and formation of the semilunar valves?
items.explanation: Cardiac neural crest cells are essential for septation of the outflow and formation of the semilunar valves.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.question: What genetic condition is associated with a deletion at chromosome 22q11?
items.explanation: A deletion at chromosome 22q11 is associated with DiGeorge syndrome.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.question: Which congenital heart defect is most commonly associated with a 'left-to-right' shunt leading to right heart dilation?
items.explanation: An atrial septal defect (ASD) is most commonly associated with a 'left-to-right' shunt, leading to right heart dilation.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the classic physical examination finding in patients with an atrial septal defect?
options: Wide, fixed splitting of the second heart sound
items.explanation: The classic physical examination finding in patients with an ASD is a wide, fixed splitting of the second heart sound.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which type of atrial septal defect is most common?
items.explanation: The secundum ASD, located in the region of the fossa ovalis, is the most common type.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is a potential complication of an unrepaired atrial septal defect?
items.explanation: Eisenmenger syndrome is a rare complication of an unrepaired atrial septal defect.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a common finding in patients with Ebstein anomaly?
options: A distinctive snapping quality of the second component of the first heart sound
Prominent 'a' waves of the jugular venous pulsations
Absence of any right atrial enlargement
items.explanation: The sail sign, a distinctive snapping quality of the second component of the first heart sound, is associated with Ebstein anomaly.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients with Ebstein anomaly may show evidence of ventricular pre-excitation?
items.explanation: Up to 20% of patients with Ebstein anomaly have evidence of ventricular pre-excitation (Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern).
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which type of VSD is most common in adults?
items.explanation: The most common location for VSDs is the membranous septum, also known as perimembranous or outlet defects.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is a characteristic murmur finding in patients with a large PDA?
options: A diastolic rumble at the apex
A continuous murmur extending from systole past the second heart sound into diastole
items.explanation: The classic murmur of a large PDA is heard best just below the left clavicle and extends from systole past the second heart sound into diastole.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which coronary artery anomaly is most commonly associated with Tetralogy of Fallot?
options: Anomalous right coronary artery from the left coronary sinus
Anomalous circumflex artery from the pulmonary trunk
Anomalous left anterior descending coronary artery from the right coronary cusp
Anomalous posterior descending coronary artery from the aorta
items.explanation: The most common coronary anomaly in Tetralogy of Fallot is an anomalous left anterior descending coronary artery arising from the right coronary cusp.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.question: What hemodynamic issue often occurs following repair of Tetralogy of Fallot?
items.explanation: Pulmonary regurgitation is common following repair of Tetralogy of Fallot and is associated with RV dilation.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.question: What is the most common reintervention in patients with repaired Tetralogy of Fallot?
items.explanation: The most common reintervention in patients with repaired Tetralogy of Fallot is pulmonary valve replacement.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.question: Which surgical procedure became the preferred treatment for D-loop Transposition of the Great Arteries in the 1980s?
items.explanation: The arterial switch operation became the preferred surgical treatment for D-loop Transposition of the Great Arteries in the 1980s.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a common association with L-loop TGA?
items.explanation: Dextrocardia is commonly associated with L-loop TGA.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients with L-loop TGA may experience complete heart block?
items.explanation: Complete heart block occurs in up to 30% of patients with L-loop TGA.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is commonly associated with coarctation of the aorta?
items.explanation: Bicuspid aortic valve is commonly associated with coarctation of the aorta, and Turner syndrome should be considered in specific clinical presentations.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 2)

items.statement: The American Board of Medical Subspecialties introduced ACHD board certification examinations in 2015.

items.subtopic: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult | PREVALENCE | THE CHANGING LANDSCAPE OF ADULT CHD
items.statement: The majority of adults with CHD are diagnosed for the first time as adults.
items.explanation: The passage states that the majority of adults with CHD were diagnosed in childhood.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult | PREVALENCE | THE CHANGING LANDSCAPE OF ADULT CHD

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.statement: Noncardiac surgery in adults with CHD does not require a comprehensive assessment.
items.explanation: Comprehensive, multispecialty assessment and care strategy review are recommended before noncardiac surgery.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.statement: Pregnant women with CHD should be counseled about the risks of morbidity and mortality during pregnancy.
items.explanation: Women at risk should be strongly counseled about significant risks of morbidity and mortality.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.statement: The heart is fully formed by the end of the third week of gestation.
items.explanation: The heart starts to form in the third week and is nearly fully formed by 8 weeks' gestation.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.statement: Children with trisomy 21 have a high chance of having congenital heart disease.
items.explanation: Children born with trisomy 21 have a 50% chance of having CHD.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.statement: A patent foramen ovale (PFO) is commonly associated with right-sided cardiac dilation.
items.explanation: A PFO persists in up to 25% of adults but is not associated with right-sided cardiac dilation.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Surgical closure is required for secundum ASDs.
items.explanation: Secundum ASDs can often be closed percutaneously with occluder devices, not necessarily requiring surgery.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Muscular VSDs often result in significant hemodynamic consequences if they persist into adulthood.
items.explanation: Muscular defects that persist into adult life are often pressure and flow restricted, resulting in no significant hemodynamic consequence.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Small VSDs usually cause symptoms of heart failure and poor somatic growth.
items.explanation: Large VSDs, not small ones, usually cause symptoms of heart failure and poor somatic growth.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with repaired Tetralogy of Fallot have a high risk of left ventricular dysfunction.
items.explanation: Left ventricular dysfunction is not common in patients with repaired Tetralogy of Fallot; right ventricular issues are more prevalent.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.statement: The arterial switch operation involves transecting the great arteries above the sinuses.
items.explanation: The arterial switch operation includes transecting the great arteries above the sinuses and repositioning them.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.statement: Patients with L-loop TGA typically present early in life due to associated defects.
items.explanation: Patients without associated defects may not present until later in life, most commonly with heart failure, TR, or conduction disease.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 2)

items.statement: The Fontan procedure involves a biventricular circulation.
items.explanation: The Fontan procedure utilizes the single ventricle to pump pulmonary venous blood through the aorta, not involving biventricular circulation.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 2)

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.assertion: Adults with CHD may not recognize subtle changes in exercise capacity.
items.reason: By the time symptoms are recognized, irreversible physiologic changes may have occurred.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why adults with CHD may not recognize changes.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.subtopic: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT
items.assertion: Anesthetic management for adults with CHD does not require knowledge of previous palliative procedures.
items.reason: Knowledge of anatomy and physiologic consequences is necessary for anesthetic management.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; anesthetic management requires knowledge of previous palliative procedures.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS FOR THE ACHD PATIENT

items.assertion: The outflow septation in the heart proceeds from proximal to distal.
items.reason: Septation of the outflow is under the influence of cardiac neural crest cells and proceeds from distal to proximal.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; septation proceeds from distal to proximal.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.assertion: Noonan syndrome is associated with a deletion at chromosome 22q11.
items.reason: DiGeorge syndrome, not Noonan syndrome, is associated with a deletion at chromosome 22q11.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; Noonan syndrome is not associated with a deletion at chromosome 22q11.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · CONGENITAL TERMINOLOGY, DEVELOPMENT, AND GENETICS

items.assertion: Primum atrial septal defects are associated with abnormal development of the AV valves.
items.reason: A primum ASD is a deficiency in the atrioventricular canal portion of the atrial septum.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Partial anomalous pulmonary venous return is commonly associated with concomitant pulmonary hypertension.
items.reason: Concomitant pulmonary hypertension can occur but is uncommon in partial anomalous pulmonary venous return.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because concomitant pulmonary hypertension is uncommon, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: The sail sign is associated with Ebstein anomaly.
items.reason: It results from the redundancy of the anterior tricuspid valve leaflet.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association of the sail sign with Ebstein anomaly.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Large PDAs can lead to elevated pulmonary vascular resistance.
items.reason: They cause left heart dilation, which increases pressure in the pulmonary circulation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the direct explanation for elevated pulmonary vascular resistance.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · SPECIFIC CHD LESIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with repaired Tetralogy of Fallot often require reintervention.
items.reason: Pulmonary regurgitation and RV dilation are common after repair, leading to adverse outcomes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients may need reintervention.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)

items.assertion: The Mustard procedure is currently the preferred surgical treatment for D-loop Transposition of the Great Arteries.
items.reason: It involves an atrial switch that relieves cyanosis by routing systemic circulation through the right ventricle.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the arterial switch operation, not the Mustard procedure, is currently preferred for D-loop TGA.
items.reference: Congenital Heart Disease in the Adult · MODERATE AND COMPLEX CHD (part 1)
